author: @kensmith5694
text: On the capacitor, leave it shorted for a long time.  There is a thing call dielectric soaking that happens on some of them.  Some electrons soak into the dielectric and don't come out when you briefly short it.  They will very slowly migrate out when there is no voltage on the capacitor and effectively charge it back up.  The voltage it goes up to will be a small fraction of the working voltage but with a working voltage of 1,000V, you can get 100V.

author: @davidgeiger
text: The only addendum is that if you only know one of the possible ways it could hurt you, telling someone just that info can give a false sense of security facilitating them running headlong into a bad situation. The safe bet is to say “this is what I know but I suggest you research it before you dive in.”

author: @audiowan
text: As a teenager i worked in a TV shop, and in those days the picture tube (CRT) had high voltage stored in it. .It essentially was a huge efficient capacitor.  Were talking 40,000 volts sometimes.  A lot of the danger came from what the persons hand / body hit as they flung into metal T.V. frame or across the room !  Requiring E.R. viset and stitches...

author: @SuperBartet
text: Great educational video. If only I had seen something like this about 30 years ago. I had a microwave that stopped working. Yes I was ignorant of the dangers inside. I took the cover off, got my multi test meter, and checked if the transformer was working. BANG my test meter vanished, it scarred the %^$£ out of me. I had no idea just how much voltage coming out of one of them is, how I did not get shocked I just don't know. I found my multi meter the other side of the room, and for some reason it never worked again.

author: @VeniceInventors
text:
I was fortunate enough to be told (and shown) that it's okay to grab ONE live wire as long as my feet are insulated and I'm not touching anything else conductive.  As a result I've never been scared of electricity, which came in handy in situations where turning the power off before fixing something wasn't an option.
The same could be said of motorcycles and even skis. They're not dangerous at all, it's losing control of them that's dangerous.

author: @cache4pat
text: Great video. You missed covering another danger from careless curiousity. Dismantling or carelessly storing the removed magnetron can be a lung hazard. ie: Magnetrons can be incredibly dangerous; while you are safe from radiation when they are not powered, the ceramic insulators can contain beryllium oxide, which can be fatal when inhaled.

author: @willhibbardii2450
text: Christmas Story... Red Ryder BB gun... You'll shoot your eye out!!! I think the question is, is how to create guards that aren't hazards themselves? As technology advances consumers are exposed to hazards that they're unaware of. Responsible assembly and maintenance also include safety to wildlife and pets. How do you teach a squirrel to look both ways before crossing the street? How do you teach a bird not to fly through a windowpane? How do you teach a cleaning person not to mix bleach and ammonia together and why? When will AI take over the world and do our thinking for us? Driverless cars, trucks, aircraft, trams and forklifts are like elevators. Push the button and it takes you to your destination. Technology is cloaked with a man behind the curtain. Fear on this training ground construct can be a good thing. We make mistakes with capacitors and rapid discharge is safe with small ones however industrial capacitors must be discharged slowly. Why? Because...??? I hate seeing the dreaded blue smoke of failure! 🤣🤣🤣

author: @1337Gareth
text:
Im in the camp that can use logic, experience and understanding to make my determination on how I approach something like this. I think I (and probably the majority of people who would be watching your videos) can operate in this way. I think though, that we have to consider the size and breadth of who could see these types of videos, and consider that people who expose themselves to it do not have the same leaning. They simply see a cool end result and a simple guide to achieve it without the understanding of what stepping out of the bounds of what they are wanting. 
It IS dangerous, and CAN kill / maim instantly, for those people a simpler warning 'Just Dont' is more effective to stop them doing themselves a mischief - if they arent the people who care or want to know more about WHY its dangerous and how to mitigate it. It is frustrating that people are not more dependent on the innate human ability to learn. Maybe by not protecting them it would serve the gene pool a measure of bleach, but that is a different topic, and a moral question for another video?

author: @tonyhardy-kp4os
text: Those plastic coverings are not for keeping your fingers from touching the terminals, they are to help prevent the voltage from jumping off the terminals to other components and case, and from jumping from terminal to terminal. It is high voltage and has tendency to jump thru the air, that is the reason for the covering on the terminals. Yes you are not supposed to touch the terminals, but that is absolutely not what the coverings are for.

author: @totherarf
text:
It's like Rock Climbing!
It is safe because people realise it isn't and take precautions to make it safe(er).

One thing I will say is that messing with fractal wood burning is dangerous because even if you research it you are changing the property of stuff. You make a fairly good insulator conduct. And it is not an obvious, in-your-face type of danger. Set your kit up and plug her in and it will be safe(ish) ...... but when you dampen that wood down suddenly that insulator turns into a killer (and you can't smell electric)! It is the type of memory we have that puts things into boxes of knowledge that is the danger! It is safe right up until the point it isn't and it looks the same, so your mind tells you to treat it the same!

I am looking forward with interest to the burner though!

author: @philbrooke-little7082
text: I get this al the time with a battery setup I talk about and that I have given all the tech reasons for, all the caveats etc. and people say ‘’you shouldn’t do that, it’s all going to go wrong, it’s dangerous, you’ll void your insurance” etc. I’m fine with that but only if the people can give me the tech reasons why I shouldn’t do it. The reaction to me asking for them is usually either silence or abuse but never actual factual reasons. All too often it is just people repeating what vested interests have said and have given no reasons for. The problem is that it is these vested interests who write the rules that stop progress.

author: @gerryjamesedwards1227
text: I keep an old insulated screwdriver attached at the tip to a 100W 2K2 resistor and a hefty alligator clip. It's handy for discharging caps without getting sparks, giving the discharge a much longer time -constant. This works fine for valve amplifier filter caps, but even that might have a bit of a spark-up with a microwave oven cap. I've had a belt off a HID light ballast capacitor, bridging the terminals of the plug with my thumb picking it up after unplugging. That was a biggy!
